text: Thank you, John. Many of you in the audience think that surgical knowledge is an oxymoron. I'm going to actually try to actually give you some ideas of what we do in the operating room besides playing music and having fun. We can start the video. So what I'm going to discuss today is basically an ileal pouch, and I particularly talk about three-stage procedures versus two-stage procedures, and what exactly are those. Let's just start that off right off the bat. This is a schematic. If you can just stop the video for a second.

On the left-hand side, this is actually the initial phase of a three-stage procedure, where actually you take just the colon out, but you leave the bottom-most portion, the rectum, in place. Is this working? No. Pardon me? Oh, the mouse is working. Okay. I don't think it's working. Okay. That's the initial stage. And then after, that's the first of three stages. The second of the three stages is actually when you take the rectum out and make an ileal J pouch showed on the right. And then the third is actually when there's an ileostomy still created and that ileostomy itself is reversed. We call that a three-stage procedure in our field. On the right-hand side is where the Initial two phases are put together. The total abdominal colectomy and the ileal pouch is connected. The main difference being that the rectum is removed and the pouch is created. And in the final phase of that two-stage procedure, the ileostomy is reversed.

The surgeon marks a right angle triangle on the patient's abdomen to plan a Pfannenstiel incision, located below the bikini line. The incision is made through the skin, revealing Scarpa's fascia and the anterior sheath of the rectus muscle. An eight-centimeter incision is created to access the abdominal cavity. A trocar is placed in the umbilicus, and another in the right lower quadrant, which will serve as the future ileostomy site. A gel port is used for insufflation to gain access to the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon begins by using a ligature instrument to fuse tissue and achieve hemostasis while dissecting the right colon. The gallbladder and liver are visible in the distance. The hepatic flexure is approached with caution to avoid the kidney, ureter, and duodenum, which is visible beneath the tissue. The colon is mobilized up to the mid-transverse colon, with attention given to preserving the ileocolic artery for maintaining pouch length.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic artery and incises the mesentery of the colon distal to it, gaining access into the mesentery. The colon is then mobilized, with careful attention to avoid injury to the duodenum and small bowel. The dissection progresses beyond the hepatic flexure, ensuring the small bowel remains unharmed.

The surgeon dissects the mesentery of the right colon, identifying the small bowel below. The dissection proceeds across the midline, with the falciform ligament visible at the top of the field. The transverse colon mesentery is being dissected, with care taken to avoid the small bowel, progressing towards the splenic flexure.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the splenic flexure, avoiding the stomach visible on the left, and continues the dissection towards the left side of the abdomen. The procedure involves hand assistance and laparoscopic techniques. The colon is mobilized, and the terminal ileum is identified by its anti-mesenteric fat, which is removed. The ileum is transected flush with the ileocecal valve, forming the efferent limb of the J pouch. The colon is then removed, completing the subtotal colectomy.

The surgeon mobilizes the ileostomy from the anterior abdominal wall, carefully preserving blood vessels and controlling minor bleeding. Adhesions in the right lower quadrant are lysed to ensure the small bowel can be positioned correctly for the ileal J pouch formation.

The surgeon closes the end of the ileostomy with a stapler, which will become the end of the ileum or the J-pouch. The healed incision is visible, marked carefully to ensure straightness for aesthetic purposes. The dissection proceeds through scar tissue down to the fascia and muscle, with a wound protector placed to reduce infection risk. The ileostomy is visible at the top left of the screen.

The surgeon uses a wound protector to improve visualization of the pelvis, ensuring the ileal J pouch can reach the anus. The J pouch is pulled down, with two loops of intestine folded back on itself, assessing its length. The rectal stump is visible, stapled off from the original surgery, with incisions made on either side to develop the suprahemorrhoidal artery. A tissue ligator is used to control hemostasis, entering the presacral space or total mesorectal plane.

The surgeon continues dissection in the mesorectal plane, which is visible as a fat plane with a small blood vessel crossing it. This anatomical plane is bloodless, reducing the risk of genitourinary dysfunction. The dissection proceeds circumferentially around the rectum, mobilizing it down to the pelvic floor. The surgeon prepares to transect the rectum while preserving the ileocolic artery, crucial for mesenteric lengthening to ensure the ileal J pouch can reach the pelvis.

The surgeon folds the J pouch back on itself, pulling it down to ensure it reaches the pelvis. Marks in the mesentery are visible, used for additional length. Stitches are placed at the apex of the J pouch for anastomosis to the anus. The efferent limb is stabilized with a stitch to prevent twisting, while the afferent limb is held up. A small enterotomy is made at the base of the J pouch, and a large surgical stapler is inserted, slightly tearing the tissue but not causing damage.

The surgeon uses a 100mm stapler to fuse the two limbs of the J-pouch together, creating a staple line visible in the middle. The stapler is reinserted to extend the J-pouch, which is completed with a length of 15 to 25 centimeters. A sponge is placed at the bottom of the J-pouch to keep it dry. The video shows the completed J-pouch, and the surgeon discusses reconstruction techniques, including double stapling to connect the J-pouch to the rectum.

The surgeon uses a Lone Star Retractor to efface the anus, providing access to the anal canal for a hand-sewn ileoanal anastomosis. The dentate line is identified and scored with a bovie to guide the removal of the mucosal lining, aiming to perform a mucosectomy.

The surgeon performs a circumferential mucosectomy by infiltrating saline to raise the mucosa from the underlying smooth muscle of the internal anal sphincter. Using forceps, the mucosa is carefully dissected and removed, preserving the sphincter function. The procedure leaves a cuff of muscle through which the J pouch is pulled, completing the pull-through technique. The cut edge of the mucosa is visible, with the intact muscle beneath.

The J pouch is brought down through the mesentery and secured with sutures at four quadrant areas to maintain its position. The posterior side of the J pouch is held by the mesentery due to length constraints. A hand-sewn ileoanal anastomosis is performed, with stitches placed onto the anal side, involving both muscle and mucosa. A diverting loop ileostomy is created, with the incision visible.
